title: Non-deterministic /sys mount behavior (rootless) means `ip netns exec` sometimes fails
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I have a network test environment that creates containers with network namespaces/interfaces within containers. I was experimenting with using rootless podman instead of docker. With the full setup I was seeing occasional permissions failures when trying to run `ip netns exec NS CMD` within the container. I was able to simplify the process until I was able to determine that the failure is caused by non-deterministic mounting behavior of the `/sys` file-system.

A couple of small scripts (and some example output) that I use to quickly test this are at https://gist.github.com/kanaka/950e307e1c8c9ccbea70de04d0267894

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Build a container image with iproute2 installed

2. Launch the container using a specific set of volume mounts that includes `$HOME/.local/share/containers/storage/overlay` plus some other volume mounts. Removing the `.local` mount means it always fails for me. Adding additional mounts changes the frequency of the behavior (ranging from 100% in either direction to about 50% failures).

3. Create a iproute2 style netns and then try to run a command using that netns (e.g. `ip netns exec NS CMD`).

**Describe the results you received:**

Some runs of the container results in `ip netns exec NS CMD` working, and other runs of the container (identical command line) will cause it to fail with a `mount of /sys failed: Operation not permitted`.

**Describe the results you expected:**

The `ip netns exec NS CMD` should always work.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

When the command works, the following sysfs related mounts appear in the container:

```
...
sysfs on /sys type sysfs (ro,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)
...
sysfs on /overlay/5089005f7b65568a0b0137a619a4fce517bacf7066e0c93cf499e87d05f9c6d2/merged/sys type sysfs (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)
```

When it doesn't work, the second mount is missing and the first read-only one usually appears later in the mount list.


**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.3.1
API Version:  3.3.1
Go Version:   go1.16.6
Built:        Wed Dec 31 18:00:00 1969
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

I have also tried building podman from source and using that with the same behavior:

```
Version:      4.0.0-dev
API Version:  4.0.0-dev
Go Version:   go1.13.8
Git Commit:   1de96f2c4c390bae29c303e2390da9e87a993d3f
Built:        Fri Oct  1 17:18:12 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: Can you provide the output of `mount` from inside the container in a container that works, and one that does not?

@mheon I have `mount` commented out in the inside test script just for that purpose.

Here is the output (including the mount output) from a case that works:

Here is the diff with container IDs removed like this:
```
diff -urp <(sed 's/[0-9a-z]\{64\}/CCC/g' mounts.broke) <(sed 's/[0-9a-z]\{64\}/CCC/g' mounts.working)
```

`+/dev/sda1 on /overlay/CCC/merged/test-ns.sh type ext4 (rw,relatime)` seems to have no counterpart in the broken example; otherwise the differences seem to be purely related to ordering (which could well be a factor). Out of curiosity, can you try with the `runc` OCI runtime as well? I *think* our logic for creating mounts in the OCI spec is deterministic, so I'm wondering where the randomness is coming from.

In addition to that mount (and the non-determinism), there is also `sysfs on /overlay/CCC/merged/sys type sysfs (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)` only in the working one and `tmpfs on /overlay/CCC/merged/dev type tmpfs (rw,nosuid,size=65536k,mode=755,uid=1000,gid=1000,inode64)` only in the broken one.

It's the additional sysfs overlay mount that seems to be the problem to me (manually mounting that in a container where it's missing makes the `ip netns exec` command work.

I'll try and run with runc instead and report back.

With `runc` I still see non-deterministic mount order that looks pretty similar to variance with `crun`. And I also see cases where the second sysfs mount (at `/overlay/.../merged/sys`) is missing.

However, the `ip netns exec NS CMD` never works with `runc` because when the second sysfs mount appears, it is always marked `ro` with `runc` rather than `rw` as it is mounted with `crun`.

Re-ordering and substituting the container ID shows that the second sysfs mount is actually the only difference (apart from ordering)

```
sysfs on /overlay/CCC/merged/sys type sysfs (ro,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime) 
```

BTW, the context here is that I am attempting to get [conlink](https://github.com/LonoCloud/conlink) working with rootless podman. conlink enables containers to be networked together with an arbitrary mininet defined network (with routers, switches, smart OVS bridges, tc policy, etc). With a few non-invasive tweaks to conlink I've gotten it to work with rootless podman. The remaining problems are: I periodically see this sysfs problem (unable to `ip netns exec`), and also that it leaves some droppings in the process tree (due to https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/11888)

the kernel doesn't allow to mount a sysfs file system in a user namespace unless there is already a writeable sysfs mount.

When it works for you, it is because sysfs is already mounted as rw at `/overlay/$CID/merged/sys`

I'd need to investigate further to be 100% sure, but I bet the fact that sometimes sysfs appears as rw could be a race condition in how the mounts are propagated in the two different mount namespaces.

Since `ip` always mount sysfs, it somehow expects to be running in the init user namespace, or to have a rw sysfs mount available (and not covered).

I'd suggest that you just use `--privileged` that forces sysfs to be mounted `rw`.

`--privileged` doesn't actually fix the problem for me. It **does** result in the `/sys` mount always being `rw`. However, the `ip netns exec NS CMD` still fails. The determining factor on whether it works is whether the second sysfs mount appears or not.

If it looks like this then the problem will still happen:

```
root@4551f56be1da:/# mount | grep sysfs
sysfs on /sys type sysfs (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)
```

If it looks like this, then `ip netns exec NS CMD` works correctly:

```
root@fadd2fa44752:/# mount | grep sysfs
sysfs on /sys type sysfs (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)
sysfs on /overlay/882c39449583767875556d147ca643874f93f5f5a30e1c678ed08bb60c847048/merged/sys type sysfs (rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)
```

So whether I use `--privileged` or `--cap-add SYS_ADMIN`, the problem still happens and the key is whether the second `sysfs` mount is setup or not. And that appears to be some sort of mount ordering issue because when there are two sysfs mounts then the plain one (`sysfs on /sys`) appears much earlier in the list of mounts. Note that with `--privileged` and `crun` it seems to break a lot less frequently that with `--privileged` and `runc`.

I see.

When ip works, it is because there is already a mounted sysfs visible (the one mounted at `/overlay/$CID/merged/sys`) so the kernel doesn't complain.

If there is only one `sysfs` mounted, then ip does "unmount("/sys"); mount("/sys")" and the mount fails since there is no `/sys` available anymore.

Not sure how this case can be addressed in the container runtime.

Would it work if you also add something like "mount -t sysfs --make-private /random/path` before calling `ip netns`?

I can confirm that doing the `mount -t sysfs --make-private /sys2` allows the `ip netns exec` command to work (even in the case where it's initially broken due to only a single read-only sysfs mount).

Interestingly that trick only needs `SYS_ADMIN` to work with `crun` whereas with `runc` it requires `--privileged`.

Thanks for confirming it.  I think the difference between runc and crun is likely some race condition when the mounts are propagated, and I don't think we can control it (either from runc/crun or from Podman).

IMO, the cleanest fix would be that `ip netns` doesn't attempt to umount the sysfs. In this way, it would work in a namespace without such sort of hacks on your side.

I will close the issue because I don't think there is anything we can do in Podman or in the OCI runtimes, but feel free to comment further, or reopen it if you disagree with the decision.
